passages.passageHtml:
[1] The electrical matter appears to be a common element in all
bodies, capable of being excited or drawn from one to another. We
electrify a body by rubbing it, as when we rub a glass tube with
silk, and find it attracts light bodies like feathers or small pieces
[5] of paper. The tube, thus rubbed, gains an electrical atmosphere
around it, which repels similar atmospheres but attracts those of
opposite kinds.

Bodies may be divided into conductors and non-conductors of
electricity. Metals, water, and moist substances conduct it readily;
[10] glass, silk, resin, and dry air do not. A charged body communicates
its electricity through conductors but not through non-conductors.
This explains why we can charge a Leyden jar—a glass vessel
coated inside and out with metal or foil—by connecting the inner
coating to a source of electricity while the outer remains grounded
[15] or insulated.

In charging the jar, we draw electrical fire from the earth or
surrounding bodies into the inner surface, while the outer surface
becomes deficient in it, creating a positive and negative state. The
jar holds this imbalance until discharged by connecting the two
[20] surfaces, when the fire rushes through the connection with great
force, producing a spark, shock, or even igniting spirits.

Experiments show that the quantity of electrical fire in a charged
jar is no greater than before charging; it is merely separated or
transferred. When we charge the jar, the electricity is not added
[25] from outside but redistributed: the inner surface gains excess
(positive), the outer loses it (negative). Upon discharge, the excess
returns to restore balance.

Points prove remarkably effective in drawing and throwing off
electrical fire. A sharp point on a charged body silently draws
[30] electricity from the air or nearby objects, while a blunt body
requires greater proximity for the same effect. This property led
me to conjecture that lightning, so destructive to buildings and
ships, is the same electrical fire as that produced in our
experiments. Thunderclouds carry vast quantities of this fire,
[35] accumulated by the rubbing of air masses or evaporation from the
sea, and when they approach the earth, the pointed tops of steeples,
trees, or chimneys may draw it down gradually and safely.
To test this, I proposed erecting iron rods with points on high
places, connected by wires to the ground, to draw the electrical fire
[40] harmlessly away. If lightning strikes such a rod, it follows the
metal conductor into the earth without damage to the structure.
Experiments with scaled models confirmed that points prevent
violent sparks by allowing gradual equalization.

Further observations reveal that electricity moves most readily
[45] along sharp edges or points, and that opposite charges attract while
like charges repel. A positively charged body repels another
positive but attracts a negative. This explains the behavior of cork
balls or threads suspended near charged tubes: they first approach,
receive charge, then fly away due to repulsion.

[50] In one experiment, two persons standing on wax (non
conductors) charged themselves from the same tube and found
they could not shock each other, as both were in the same state; yet
each could produce a shock by touching a third person who was
grounded.

[1] The primary facts of birth and death in any social group make
education necessary. There is the contrast between the immaturity
of new members—the group's future representatives—and the maturity
of adults who hold the group's knowledge and customs. The young
[5] must not only be kept alive physically but initiated into the
interests, purposes, skills, and practices of the mature;
otherwise, the group's distinctive life ends. Even in a simple
tribe, adults' achievements surpass what the young could manage
alone. As civilization advances, the gap widens between innate
[10] capacities and cultural standards. Mere physical growth or survival
skills cannot reproduce group life. Deliberate effort is required
to make the young aware of and interested in social aims and
habits. Education alone bridges this divide.

Society exists through transmission and communication. There
[15] is more than a verbal link among "common," "community," and "
communication". People form a community by sharing aims, beliefs,
aspirations, and knowledge—a common understanding. These cannot
pass like physical objects; they require communication that builds
similar emotional and intellectual responses. Communication shares
[20] experience until it becomes mutual possession, altering
thev dispositions of all involved.

Life strives to continue through constant renewal; it is self
renewing. Nutrition and reproduction sustain physiological life;
education sustains social life through transmission via
[25] communication—a process of sharing experience to make it common.
This modifies participants' outlooks. The deeper value of human
association lies in improving experience quality, dearest in
dealings with the young. Every social arrangement educates, but
this becomes intentional when older and younger associate. As
[30] societies grow complex, formal teaching expands. Yet increased
formal instruction risks separating school learning from direct
life experience—a danger heightened today by rapid knowledge growth
and technical skills.

A community sustains itself through self-renewal via the
[35] educational growth of its immature members. Children enter school
with "minds"—knowledge and judgment habits formed by using things
in relation to others' uses. Mind is the power to understand
objects by their shared use; a socialized mind grasps them in
joint situations. Environment means more than surroundings; it
[40] includes medium—tools, language, customs—that shape activities.
Social environment educates by directing responses toward shared
ends.

Intentional education selects environments to promote desired
growth. Schools provide special environments, but daily life
[45] educates profoundly through participation. A savage group
perpetuates savagery, a civilized one civilization, because daily
conditions form dispositions. Only joint activity—referring one's
use of tools and materials to others—achieves social direction of
habits. Imitation alone is mechanical; true education involves
[50] conscious reference to shared meaning.

Education as a social function varies with group quality.
Democratic society demands education fostering wide shared
interests, reliance on mutual recognition for control, free
interaction across groups, and continuous social readjustment.
[55] Democracy is more than government; it is conjoint communicated
experience—a mode of associated living emphasizing participation
and growth through interaction.

[1] The ways of behaving and thinking that are common in a society
arise from efforts to meet needs. At first, these ways are simply
habits of individuals or customs of the group. They come into
existence through trial and error, as people experiment to find what
[5] satisfies their interests most easily. Over time, repetition turns
these into fixed patterns. The young absorb them through imitation,
tradition, and the pressure of those around them. No one deliberately
invents them; they grow unconsciously. These habitual ways of doing
things may be called "folkways." They include ordinary practices such
[10] as greetings, dress, manners at meals, or methods of work. At the
start, folkways carry no strong sense of right or wrong. They are
simply the ways things are done. People follow them because it is
convenient and because deviation draws mild disapproval or ridicule.

Violating a folkway might make one seem odd or rude, but it does not
[15] provoke deep moral outrage. Some folkways, however, gain greater
importance. When they connect closely to the welfare of the group—
when they seem essential to survival, health, or prosperity—they take
on a moral quality. People begin to believe that these ways are not
just convenient but right and necessary. They judge them as good or
[20] bad, wise or foolish. Such strengthened folkways become "mores." The
mores include ideas about what is decent, honorable, or virtuous.

They enforce themselves through social approval for conformity and
disapproval, shame, or even ostracism for violation. The mores are
coercive. They impose themselves on individuals with little room for
[25] choice. One who defies them risks being seen as wicked or dangerous.

In primitive societies, the mores often blend with superstitions or
religious beliefs, making them even stronger. For example, certain
foods may be forbidden not because they harm health but because
tradition holds they anger spirits or bring bad luck. In more advanced
[30] groups, mores govern marriage, property, family duties, and justice.
They define what is polite, just, or moral in daily life. The
distinction between folkways and mores is one of degree rather than
kind. A folkway may rise to the level of a more if circumstances make
it seem vital to group well-being. Conversely, a more may weaken into
[35] a mere folkway if its original importance fades. Both originate in
the same process: repeated actions that prove useful become expected,
then obligatory. Mores always carry a judgment that they promote
societal welfare. They are regarded as the "right" ways because they
are traditional and appear to work. People rarely question them
[40] rationally; instead, they accept them on faith. Attempts to change
mores by argument or law often fail because they run against deep
seated habits and beliefs. Legislation that contradicts the mores is
likely to be evaded or ignored. In every society, the mores regulate
conduct far more powerfully than formal laws. Laws codify what the
[45] mores already demand, or they try to introduce new rules that the
mores have not yet accepted. When laws align with the mores, they
reinforce them; when they conflict, the mores prevail. History shows
that great changes in customs come slowly, through shifts in
experience and conditions, not through sudden decrees. Folkways and
[50] mores illustrate how human behavior is shaped by the group rather
than by individual reason alone. They explain why societies differ so
widely in their practices and why outsiders often find foreign customs
strange or irrational. Yet within each society, these ways feel
natural and inevitable. They form the unseen framework that guides
[55] life from day to day, ensuring order without constant deliberation
or force.

[1] I read the other day some verses written by an eminent
painter which were original and not conventional. The soul always
hears an admonition in such lines, let the subject be what it may.

The sentiment they instil is of more value than any thought they
[5] may contain. To believe your own thought, to believe that what
is true for you in your private heart is true for all men,—that is
genius. Speak your latent conviction, and it shall be the universal
sense; for the inmost in due time becomes the outmost,—and our
first thought is rendered back to us by the trumpets of the Last
[10] Judgment. Familiar as the voice of the mind is to each, the
highest merit we ascribe to Moses, Plato, and Milton is, that they
set at naught books and traditions, and spoke not what men but
what they thought. A man should learn to detect and watch that
gleam of light which flashes across his mind from within, more
[15] than the lustre of the firmament of bards and sages. Yet he
dismisses without notice his thought, because it is his. In every
work of genius we recognize our own rejected thoughts: they come
back to us with a certain alienated majesty. Great works of art
have no more affecting lesson for us than this. They teach us to
[20] abide by our spontaneous impression with good- humored
inflexibility then most when the whole cry of voices is on the other
side. Else, to- morrow a stranger will say with masterly good sense
precisely what we have thought and felt all the time, and we shall
be forced to take with shame our own opinion from another.

[25]There is a time in every man's education when he arrives at the
conviction that envy is ignorance; that imitation is suicide; that he
must take himself for better, for worse, as his portion; that though
the wide universe is full of good, no kernel of nourishing corn can
[30] come to him but through his toil bestowed on that plot of ground
which is given to him to till. The power which resides in him is
new in nature, and none but he knows what that is which he can
do, nor does he know until he has tried. Not for nothing one face,
one character, one fact, makes much impression on him, and
[35] another none. This sculpture in the memory is not without
preestablished harmony. The eye was placed where one ray should
fall, that it might testify of that particular ray. We but half express
ourselves, and are ashamed of that divine idea which each of us
represents. It may be safely trusted as proportionate and of good
[40] issues, so it be faithfully imparted, but God will not have his work
made manifest by cowards. A man is relieved and gay when he has
put his heart into his work and done his best; but what he has said
or done otherwise, shall give him no peace. It is a deliverance
which does not deliver. In the attempt his genius deserts him; no
[45] muse befriends; no invention, no hope.

Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string. Accept the
place the divine providence has found for you, the society of your
contemporaries, the connection of events. Great men have always
done so, and confided themselves childlike to the genius of their
[50] age, betraying their perception that the absolutely trustworthy was
seated at their heart, working through their hands, predominating
in all their being. And we are now men, and must accept in the
highest mind the same transcendent destiny; and not minors and
invalids in a protected corner, not cowards fleeing before a
[55] revolution, but guides, redeemers, and benefactors, obeying the
Almighty effort, and advancing on Chaos and the Dark.

What pretty oracles nature yields us on this text, in the face and
behaviour of children, babes, and even brutes! That divided and
rebel mind, that distrust of a sentiment because our arithmetic has
[60] computed the strength and means opposed to our purpose, these
have not. Their mind being whole, their eye is as yet unconquered,
and when we look in their faces, we are disconcerted. Infancy
conforms to nobody: all conform to it, so that one babe commonly
makes four or five out of the adults who prattle and play to it. So
[65] God has armed youth and puberty and manhood no less with its
own piquancy and charm, and made it enviable and gracious and
its claims not to be put by, if it will stand by itself. Do not think the
youth has no force, because he cannot speak to you and me. Hark!
in the next room his voice is sufficiently clear and emphatic. It
[70] seems he knows how to speak to his contemporaries. Bashful or
bold, then, he will know how to make us seniors very unnecessary.

The nonchalance of boys who are sure of a dinner, and would
disdain as much as a lord to do or say aught to conciliate one, is the
healthy attitude of human nature. A boy is in the parlour what the
[75] pit is in the playhouse; independent, irresponsible, looking out
from his corner on such people and facts as pass by, he tries and
sentences them on their merits, in the swift, summary way of boys,
as good, bad, interesting, silly, eloquent, troublesome. He cumbers
himself never about consequences, about interests: he gives an
[80] independent, genuine verdict. You must court him: he does not
court you. But the man is, as it were, clapped into jail by his
consciousness. As soon as he has once acted or spoken with eclat,
he is a committed person, watched by the sympathy or the hatred
of hundreds, whose affections must now enter into his account.
[85] There is no Lethe for this. Ah, that he could pass again into his
neutrality! Who can thus avoid all pledges, and having observed,
observe again from the same unaffected, unbiased, uncribable,
unaffrighted innocence, must always be formidable.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the main purpose of the fourth paragraph (lines 22-27) is to:

options.A: introduce the practical application of a theory by describing the function of the Leyden jar.
options.B: contrast the behavior of electrical fire in conductors versus non-conductors.
options.C: correct a potential misconception about the nature of the electrical charge in a Leyden jar.
options.D: provide evidence for the existence of positive and negative electrical states.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph begins with "Experiments show that the quantity of electrical fire in a charged jar is no greater than before charging; it is merely separated or transferred." This directly challenges the intuitive idea that charging a jar involves adding something to it. The rest of the paragraph reinforces this by explaining the process as a redistribution, not a creation, of electrical fire. Therefore, its purpose is to clarify a point and correct a misconception. Option A describes the purpose of the third paragraph. Option B is a broader topic covered in the second paragraph. Option D is a result of the explanation but not the primary purpose of the paragraph itself.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements best captures the narrator's perspective on the relationship between the small-scale experiments described and the natural phenomenon of lightning?

options.A: The experiments with points and sparks are entertaining diversions, but lightning is a force of a fundamentally different and more mysterious nature.
options.B: The destructive power of lightning suggests that it operates according to principles entirely opposite to the gentle attractions seen in the lab.
options.C: The behavior of electricity in experiments provides a reliable model for understanding the behavior of lightning in the atmosphere.
options.D: While the experiments confirm the existence of electrical fire, they offer no clues as to how it might accumulate in thunderclouds.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator explicitly states, "This property led me to conjecture that lightning... is the same electrical fire as that produced in our experiments." He then proposes a practical application (lightning rods) based on this conjecture and notes that scaled-model experiments confirmed his ideas about points. This demonstrates a clear perspective that the principles observed in small-scale experiments are directly applicable.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, what is a necessary condition for two charged bodies to attract one another?

options.A: They must both be conductors of electricity.
options.B: They must be in opposite electrical states.
options.C: They must have been charged by a glass tube rubbed with silk.
options.D: They must be separated by a non-conductor like dry air.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage explicitly states in lines 45-47, "opposite charges attract while like charges repel. A positively charged body repels another positive but attracts a negative." This directly answers the question. Attraction depends on the charges being opposite. The other conditions are not presented as necessary for attraction itself.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred from the passage that if a person standing on a wax cake touches a charged Leyden jar and receives a shock, the wax cake is serving primarily as a(n):

options.A: conductor, to facilitate the flow of electricity from the jar to the person.
options.B: source of electrical fire, to replenish what is lost by the jar.
options.C: point, to draw the electrical fire silently from the jar.
options.D: insulator, to prevent the charge from dissipating into the ground.

questions.explanationHtml: The final paragraph describes two persons standing on wax (a nonconductor) so they can be charged. In the second paragraph (lines 9-11), the author states that non-conductors do not allow electricity to pass. If the person were standing on the ground (a conductor), any charge from the jar would flow through them into the earth.

questions.questionHtml: Both the description of the Leyden jar experiment (lines 12-21) and the lightning rod proposal (lines 38-43) are told from the point of view of a narrator who:

options.A: expresses skepticism about the practical applications of theoretical knowledge.
options.B: focuses on the destructive potential of uncontrolled electrical fire.
options.C: reasons from observed electrical principles to explain or create effects.
options.D: prioritizes the role of non-conductors in making the flow of electricity.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator consistently uses experimental observations, such as the behavior of points and the Leyden jar, to propose practical applications like lightning rods, showing that he reasons from observed principles. Both descriptions illustrate this approach.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the seventh paragraph (lines 44-49) is that:

options.A: charged objects will always repel each other after coming into contact.
options.B: the principles of attraction and repulsion govern the observable behavior of objects in electrical experiments.
options.C: cork balls are the most effective material for demonstrating the presence of an electrical charge.
options.D: a positively charged body is more powerful than a negatively charged body.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph begins by stating "Further observations reveal that electricity moves most readily along sharp edges or points, and that opposite charges attract while like charges repel." It then immediately provides an example of how this principle explains a common experimental observation: the behavior of cork balls. The main point is to connect the fundamental rules of attraction/repulsion to observable phenomena. Options A and D are contradicted or not supported by the text. Option C is too specific and not the main point.

questions.questionHtml: The author's claim that lightning is "the same electrical fire as that produced in our experiments" (lines 32-34) would be best supported if which of the following were observed?

options.A: A pointed metal rod on a building is sometimes struck by lightning, causing damage.
options.B: During a thunderstorm, a ship's mast without a metal tip is more likely to be struck than one with a metal tip.
options.C: A sharp metal point on a charged electroscope in a lab draws electricity from the air more effectively than a blunt rod.
options.D: Thunderclouds are formed by the rapid evaporation of sea water.

questions.explanationHtml: The author's claim is based on the idea that the electrical fire in lightning follows the same physical principles as the electrical fire in his lab. The most direct support for this would be evidence that a principle observed in the lab (points effectively draw electricity) also holds true for atmospheric electricity. If a sharp point draws lab electricity better, and lightning is the same thing, then a sharp point should also draw lightning's electricity. The proposal for lightning rods is based on this very principle. Option A would contradict the claim about points drawing fire safely. Option B is a prediction based on the claim, not evidence that supports the fundamental similarity. Option D addresses the origin of the fire in clouds, not its fundamental nature.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred that the author of the passage would view the idea of placing a large, rounded metal ball at the top of a building for lightning protection as:

options.A: a wise choice, as its size would allow it to attract more lightning.
options.B: an ineffective method, as a blunt object requires much closer proximity to draw the electrical fire.
options.C: a practical alternative, if a sharp point is not available.
options.D: a dangerous mistake, as the metal would conduct the lightning into the building.

questions.explanationHtml: The author explicitly states in lines 29-31 that "a sharp point... silently draws electricity... while a blunt body requires greater proximity for the same effect." From this, one can infer that he would consider a blunt object (like a rounded ball) to be a less effective and therefore inferior method for the intended purpose of drawing lightning. The principle suggests it would need to be extremely close (i.e., almost be struck) to work. Option D is incorrect because any conductor, pointed or blunt, would conduct lightning; the author's point is about how effectively the object draws the strike in the first place.

questions.questionHtml: According to the passage, what happens to two people who have both been charged by the same source while standing on a non-conducting surface?

options.A: They are able to deliver a powerful shock to each other.
options.B: They are unable to shock each other because they share the same electrical state.
options.C: They can shock each other only by touching through a conductor.
options.D: They can shock a grounded person but not each other.

questions.explanationHtml: The final paragraph states that two persons on wax charged from the same tube "could not shock each other, as both were in the same state; yet each could produce a shock by touching a third person who was grounded." This directly matches option D. Options A and B are incorrect because they are only partially correct (they can't shock each other, but they can shock a grounded person). Option C is not supported by the text in this specific scenario.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following quotations from the passage provides the strongest evidence for the claim that genuine education cannot occur through solitary activity?

options.A: The young must not only be kept alive physically but initiated into the interests, purposes, skills, and practices of the mature (lines 4-6).
options.B: Communication shares experience until it becomes mutual possession, altering the dispositions of all involved (lines 19-21).
options.C: Only joint activity—referring one's use of tools and materials to others—achieves social direction of habits (lines 47-48).
options.D: Democratic society demands education fostering wide shared interests, reliance on mutual recognition for control (lines 52-53).

questions.explanationHtml: This question requires identifying the line that most directly supports the idea that genuine education cannot happen through solitary activity. Option C explicitly states that 'only joint activity' (emphasis added) achieves the social direction of habits, directly ruling out solitary activity as sufficient for genuine education. Option A describes what education must accomplish but doesn't specify the mechanism. Option B describes the effect of communication but doesn't contrast it with solitary activity. Option D describes democratic education's characteristics but is too narrow and specific to address the general claim.

questions.questionHtml: As it is used in line 49, 'imitation' most nearly means:

options.A: repetition of actions without understanding their shared purpose
options.B: the natural process by which children learn language and customs
options.C: the primary mechanism of education in primitive societies
options.D: conscious reference to the meaning of joint activities

questions.explanationHtml: The full sentence provides the context: 'Imitation alone is mechanical; true education involves conscious reference to shared meaning' (lines 49-50). The author contrasts 'imitation' with 'conscious reference to shared meaning' and characterizes it as 'mechanical.' Therefore, imitation means performing actions without the conscious understanding of their social purpose—mere repetition without comprehension. Option B might describe imitation but misses the negative connotation Dewey assigns to it. Option C is unsupported by the passage. Option D describes the opposite of what imitation means in this context.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the primary purpose of the third paragraph (lines 22-33) is to:

options.A: illustrate how formal education systems inevitably fail by becoming disconnected from everyday life.
options.B: argue that the physical renewal of a society through birth is more fundamental than its social renewal through education.
options.C: establish a parallel between biological life and social life, both of which depend on a process of transmission for continuity.
options.D: warn against the dangers of formal instruction becoming separated from direct life experience.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph begins by stating 'Life strives to continue through constant renewal.' It then distinguishes between two types of renewal: biological ('Nutrition and reproduction sustain physiological life') and social ('education sustains social life through transmission via communication'). The entire paragraph develops this parallel between biological and social processes, with communication and shared experience being the mechanism for social transmission. Option D presents a subtle, text-based distractor: the paragraph does mention the danger of separation (lines 30-33), but this is a secondary observation within the larger purpose of establishing the biological-social parallel, not the paragraph's primary purpose. The author is not arguing for the primacy of one type of renewal over the other (B), and the paragraph's purpose is broader than illustrating inevitable failure (A).

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements best captures how the author of the passage views the relationship between a child's mind and their social environment?

options.A: A child's mind is a blank slate upon which the social environment must inscribe its knowledge and customs for the child to become a functional member of society.
options.B: The child's mind is inherently social, developing its capacity to understand the world through participation in shared activities and the use of common tools.
options.C: The social environment is a potential threat to a child's innate, individualistic mode of thought, which must be protected through intentional education.
options.D: A child's mind develops independently of the social environment, and it is only upon entering school that social influences begin to shape it.

questions.explanationHtml: This is directly supported by lines 35-39. The author states that children enter school with minds already formed 'by using things in relation to others' uses.' He then defines mind as the 'power to understand objects by their shared use' and says a 'socialized mind grasps them in joint situations.' This refutes the idea of a blank slate (A) and instead posits that the mind is fundamentally shaped by and for social interaction. The author never suggests the social environment threatens the mind (C) or that the mind develops independently of it (D).

questions.questionHtml: According to the author, the 'deeper value of human association' (line 26) is most directly realized in:

options.A: the establishment of complex governments and legal systems.
options.B: the transmission of technical skills from one generation to the next.
options.C: the interactions between the older and younger members of a society.
options.D: the creation of a 'common understanding' through verbal communication.

questions.explanationHtml: The relevant sentence (lines 26-28) states: 'The deeper value of human association lies in improving experience quality, dearest in dealings with the young.' The phrase 'dearest in dealings with the young' directly points to intergenerational interactions as the most precious or most significant manifestation of this value. While governments (A), technical transmission (B), and communication (D) are elements of his argument, they do not directly answer the question of where this value is most directly realized according to the specific text cited.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred from the passage that the author would most likely consider an educational system based primarily on rote memorization and individual competition to be inadequate because it:

options.A: focuses too much on the physical survival skills of the young.
options.B: fails to modify the dispositions of all involved through shared experience.
options.C: is a method best suited for simple tribes, not advanced civilizations.
options.D: relies on the imitation of adults, which the author deems purely mechanical.

questions.explanationHtml: The core of Dewey's argument is that true education is a social process of 'transmission via communication' (line 24) that 'modifies participants' outlooks' (line 26) and involves 'conscious reference to shared meaning' (lines 49-50). A system based on rote memorization and individual competition would lack this shared, communicative, and transformative element. It would be a mechanical process of individual acquisition, not a social one of mutual modification. While Dewey criticizes mechanical imitation (D), his deeper concern is that such methods fail to create the shared experience that modifies dispositions.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the sixth paragraph (lines 42-50) is that:

options.A: schooling is a more effective educational tool than participation in daily life.
options.B: savage groups perpetuate themselves through imitation, whereas civilized groups use intentional education.
options.C: genuine social direction of an individual's habits is achieved through participation in joint activities with shared purposes.
options.D: the primary function of schools is to select special environments that isolate children from the corrupting influences of daily life.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph contrasts the mechanical nature of 'imitation alone' (lines 48-49) with the true path to education. It explicitly states, 'Only joint activity—referring one's use of tools and materials to others—achieves social direction of habits' (lines 47-48). This is the central claim of the paragraph. The other options misinterpret the role of schools (A, D) or misrepresent the distinction between savage and civilized groups (B), which is used as an example of environmental influence, not a point about educational methods.

questions.questionHtml: The author's claim that 'increased formal instruction risks separating school learning from direct life experience' (lines 30-32) would be best supported by which of the following hypothetical scenarios?

options.A: A student excels at solving hypothetical math problems in class but struggles to calculate the correct change during a real-world transaction.
options.B: A child learns to speak their native language fluently by listening to and interacting with their family at home.
options.C: An apprentice learns a trade by working alongside a master craftsman, using the same tools and materials in a real workshop.
options.D: A young person from a rural area moves to a city for university and encounters new social customs and technologies.

questions.explanationHtml: This scenario perfectly illustrates the 'gap' or 'separation' between school learning and life experience. The student has acquired a formal skill (solving math problems) but is unable to apply it in a direct, meaningful life context (making a purchase). This demonstrates the 'risk' of separation that Dewey identifies. The other options describe learning integrated with life (B, C) or a change of environment (D), which do not demonstrate the risk of separation—in fact, they show the opposite of what Dewey warns against.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred that the author views the potential for separating school learning from life experience as a 'danger' (line 32) primarily because it could:

options.A: slow the pace of technological innovation and knowledge growth.
options.B: undermine the process by which individuals are initiated into shared social aims.
options.C: place too much emphasis on the maturity of adults and not enough on the immaturity of the young.
options.D: force societies to rely on nutrition and reproduction as their only means of self-renewal.

questions.explanationHtml: The entire passage argues that the purpose of education is to initiate the young into the 'interests, purposes, skills, and practices of the mature' (lines 5-6) to ensure the group's life continues. If school learning is separate from life experience, it cannot effectively transmit these shared social aims. The 'danger' is that this core function of social self-renewal will fail. The other options are not supported; the author doesn't argue the danger would slow innovation (A) or prioritize adult maturity (C), nor would it make biological renewal the only method (D), just that social renewal would be compromised.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the primary purpose of the lines 24-30 ("They impose themselves... family duties, and justice.") is to:

options.A: provide a specific example of a folkway that has lost its importance.
options.B: illustrate the coercive nature of mores by describing their consequences.
options.C: argue that mores in advanced societies are less powerful than in primitive ones.
options.D: explain the process by which a folkway gradually evolves into a more.

questions.explanationHtml: Lines 24-30 directly follow the statement that mores are "coercive" and "impose themselves on individuals." The paragraph then elaborates on this by first stating the risk for those who defy them ("seen as wicked or dangerous") and then providing concrete examples of the domains mores govern in different societies (superstitions in primitive groups, institutions like marriage and justice in advanced groups). This serves to illustrate and give evidence for their powerful, coercive nature.

questions.questionHtml: According to the passage, what is the initial distinguishing characteristic of folkways at the moment they first arise?

options.A: They are enforced by the threat of legal punishment.
options.B: They are viewed as essential for the group's survival.
options.C: They are deliberate inventions designed to maintain order.
options.D: They carry no strong sense of moral right or wrong.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage states this explicitly in lines 10-12: "At the start, folkways carry no strong sense of right or wrong. They are simply the ways things are done." The other options describe characteristics of mores (B), formal laws (A), or are directly contradicted by the text (C).

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that an individual who refuses to eat a certain food solely because their grandparents also refused to eat it is primarily acting according to:

options.A: a rational calculation of the food's health benefits.
options.B: a folkway that has not yet gained moral significance.
options.C: a more that is deeply rooted in tradition.
options.D: a formal law that prohibits the consumption of that food.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage describes mores as being accepted on faith because they are "traditional and appear to work" (lines 38-40) and often blend with superstitions, such as food taboos (lines 26-29). The individual's reason ("because their grandparents also refused") is based on tradition and faith, not rational analysis, which aligns with how people adhere to mores. This specific behavior matches the example of a food taboo given for mores, not the more casual convenience of a folkway.

questions.questionHtml: Based on the passage, the author (William Graham Sumner) would most likely agree with which of the following statements about the relationship between law and social change?

options.A: Laws are the most effective tool for quickly reforming outdated customs.
options.B: Legislators should always prioritize creating new laws to challenge existing mores.
options.C: Laws that are not supported by the underlying mores are unlikely to be effective.
options.D: The primary function of law is to introduce new rules that the mores have not yet accepted.

questions.explanationHtml: The author directly states this in lines 41-43: "Legislation that contradicts the mores is likely to be evaded or ignored." and reinforces it by saying "when they conflict, the mores prevail" (lines 46-47). This clearly indicates a belief that laws without the backing of mores are ineffective. Option D is mentioned as a function of law, but it is one that often fails, so the author wouldn't "agree" with it as a primary or successful function.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following quotations, if found in a historical document, would best support the passage author's claim that "the mores regulate conduct far more powerfully than formal laws" (lines 43-44)?

options.A: "The new statute requiring all citizens to wear a safety helmet was met with widespread and successful defiance, as it clashed with our long-held traditions of personal freedom."
options.B: "The ancient code of law, carved in stone, prescribed severe penalties for theft, and these penalties were carried out by the king's soldiers without exception."
options.C: "Our village council has enacted a rule that all houses must be painted white, and any villager who paints their house a different color will be fined."
options.D: "The younger generation has begun to question the old ways, finding them irrational and preferring to follow the new laws established by the federal government."

questions.explanationHtml: The author's claim is that mores (traditional customs) have more power than formal laws. Option A provides direct evidence for this by describing a situation where a formal law is ineffective ("met with... successful defiance") precisely because it clashes with a "long- held tradition" (a more). The other options either show a law being effectively enforced (B, C) or show a shift away from mores towards laws, which would contradict the author's claim about their relative power.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the last paragraph (lines 48-56, "History shows... or force.") is that:

options.A: the slow evolution of customs is a primary cause of conflict between different societies.
options.B: folkways and mores create an invisible structure that ensures social order organically.
options.C: societies with irrational customs are doomed to change through sudden, forceful decrees.
options.D: individual reason is the most powerful force in shaping a society's practices.

questions.explanationHtml: The final paragraph synthesizes the passage's main ideas. It states that folkways and mores show how group behavior is shaped, explains why societies differ, and concludes by saying these customs form "the unseen framework that guides life... ensuring order without constant deliberation or force." This directly captures the paragraph's main point about their function in creating organic social order.

questions.questionHtml: According to the passage, how does a more originally come into existence?

options.A: It is deliberately invented by a society's leaders to maintain control.
options.B: It begins as a folkway that later gains moral importance by seeming vital to the group's welfare.
options.C: It is a sudden moral revelation that is immediately accepted by the entire society.
options.D: It is created when a formal law is successfully enforced over a long period of time.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage describes the transition in lines 15-20 and 32-34: "Some folkways, however, gain greater importance. When they connect closely to the welfare of the group... they take on a moral quality. Such strengthened folkways become 'mores.'" This outlines a clear progression from folkway to more based on perceived importance to group welfare.

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred that the author uses the word "unconsciously" in line 8 primarily to emphasize that folkways:

options.A: are developed through deliberate planning and rational thought.
options.B: are created unintentionally through the gradual repetition of useful actions.
options.C: remain hidden from the view of sociologists and historians.
options.D: are practices that individuals follow while they are asleep or not paying attention.

questions.explanationHtml: The sentence structure (lines 6-8) is key: "No one deliberately invents them; they grow unconsciously." The word "unconsciously" is set in direct opposition to "deliberately invents." This, combined with the earlier description of them arising from "trial and error" and "repetition," implies they are an unintentional byproduct of group behavior, not a planned creation.

questions.questionHtml: The author most likely includes the example of "certain foods may be forbidden" (lines 27-29) in order to:

options.A: demonstrate how mores in primitive societies are often reinforced by superstition and religious belief.
options.B: provide a contrast to the types of mores, like property and justice, found in more advanced groups.
options.C: suggest that food taboos are the only type of more that can be found in every known human society.
options.D: argue that food taboos are irrational and should be discarded in favor of modern nutritional science.

questions.explanationHtml: The example is introduced directly after the statement that in primitive societies, mores "often blend with superstitions or religious beliefs, making them even stronger" (lines 25-27). The food taboo is a concrete illustration of this point, showing how a prohibition is based on spiritual belief ("anger spirits") rather than practical concerns ("harm health"), thereby making the more stronger.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the main purpose of the second paragraph (lines 25-45) is to:

options.A: provide a practical method for achieving fame and recognition from one's peers.
options.B: argue that self-trust is essential because a person's potential is unique and can only be realized through personal effort.
options.C: illustrate the painful consequences of imitating the successful strategies of historical figures like Moses and Plato.
options.D: advocate for a life of solitude to better hear the 'divine idea' that each person represents.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph argues that a person must accept their own 'portion' and that real power comes from working one's own 'plot of ground.' It states that a person's potential is 'new in nature' and unknown until tried. This directly supports the core idea that trusting and acting on one's own unique self is essential, not just a nice idea.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements best captures how the narrator of the passage feels about adults in comparison to boys?

options.A: Adults are to be pitied because they have lost the nonchalance and independent judgment that boys possess.
options.B: Adults are fortunate because their consciousness allows them to build meaningful connections with others.
options.C: Adults are more formidable than boys because they have experience and have proven themselves in society.
options.D: Adults are wise to discard the 'unbiased, uncribbed' innocence of youth in favor of a committed, principled life.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator describes the boy's state as the 'healthy attitude of human nature' and contrasts it with the man who is 'clapped into jail by his consciousness' and cannot return to that state of neutrality. This shows the narrator views the adult condition as a loss of freedom and genuine judgment, making it pitiable compared to the boy's.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, the highest merit we ascribe to figures like Moses, Plato, and Milton is that they:

options.A: composed works that were both highly original and strictly conventional.
options.B: rejected books and traditions to speak their own thoughts.
options.C: had thoughts that were universally understood in their own time.
options.D: wrote primarily about the importance of trusting in divine providence.

questions.explanationHtml: This is stated directly in lines 10-13. The author says their 'highest merit' is that 'they set at naught books and traditions, and spoke not what men but what they thought.'

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, a man who has not put his whole heart into his work, and has said or done things otherwise, will experience:

options.A: a sense of relief and gaiety from being unburdened.
options.B: a renewed connection to his muse and fresh invention.
options.C: a feeling of shame when he hears his own ideas from another.
options.D: a lack of peace and a sense of false deliverance.

questions.explanationHtml: Lines 41-45 state that a man is 'relieved and gay' only when he has done his best. It then contrasts this by saying if he acts otherwise, it 'shall give him no peace. It is a deliverance which does not deliver.'

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that the narrator believes the "nonchalance of boys" (line 72) is a "healthy attitude" primarily because they:

options.A: are unconcerned with securing their next meal, unlike adults.
options.B: have not yet learned the social conventions that constrain adult judgment.
options.C: are naturally more eloquent and interesting than adults.
options.D: possess a stronger connection to divine providence than adults do.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator praises boys for their independence and for judging people on their merits 'in the swift, summary way,' unburdened by 'consequences' or 'interests.' This contrasts with adults, who are 'committed' and watched by others. The inference is that this healthy attitude stems from a lack of social conditioning that corrupts pure judgment.

questions.questionHtml: This passage is told from the point of view of a narrator who:

options.A: struggles to reconcile his own ideas with the prevailing philosophies of his time.
options.B: fondly recalls the carefree nature of his own boyhood as a source of comfort.
options.C: passionately advocates for individualism and trusting one's inner voice above all external influences.
options.D: offers a critical analysis of why some people achieve greatness while others do not.

questions.explanationHtml: The entire passage is a forceful argument for self-reliance. The narrator consistently urges the reader to trust their own thoughts (lines 5-7), abide by their own impressions (lines 19-20), and to 'Trust thyself' (line 46). The point of view is that of an advocate and guide, not just an analyst or a person reminiscing.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the last paragraph (lines 56-88) is that:

options.A: adults are often jealous of the freedom they see in children and youths.
options.B: the uninhibited and independent nature of youth serves as a powerful model for how adults should live.
options.C: boys are rude and inconsiderate, which is why they must be courted by adults.
options.D: the primary difference between youth and adulthood is the ability to be eloquent and emphatic.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph uses the behavior of children and boys as examples ('pretty oracles') of the self-trust the author advocates. Their 'unconquered' eye, nonchalance, and independent judgment are held up as a 'healthy attitude' that adults have lost. The point is to show adults what they should aspire to be.

questions.questionHtml: The author claims that a person often dismisses their own thought 'because it is his' (line 16). What evidence from the passage would best support this claim?

options.A: 'The soul always hears an admonition in such lines, let the subject be what it may.' (lines 2-3)
options.B: 'In every work of genius we recognize our own rejected thoughts: they come back to us with a certain alienated majesty.' (lines 16-18)
options.C: 'The eye was placed where one ray should fall, that it might testify of that particular ray.' (lines 36-37)
options.D: 'He cumbers himself never about consequences, about interests: he gives an independent, genuine verdict.' (lines 78-80)

questions.explanationHtml: This statement directly proves the author's point. It shows that we reject our own thoughts, only to recognize them later in the work of a genius. The fact that we recognize them as our own demonstrates that we dismissed them solely for being ours, and their appearance in a great work gives them back their 'alienated majesty.'

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred that the author uses the phrase 'clapped into jail by his consciousness' (lines 81-82) mainly to emphasize that an adult's:

options.A: awareness of society's judgment restricts his freedom to act and judge independently.
options.B: moral conscience prevents him from committing the same irresponsible acts as a boy.
options.C: personal achievements serve as a foundation for a secure and stable life.
options.D: social connections with hundreds of people provide him with a strong support network.

questions.explanationHtml: The 'jail' is his 'consciousness' of being 'watched by the sympathy or the hatred of hundreds.' This awareness forces him to consider their 'affections' in his 'account,' restricting his ability to be the independent, unbiased judge that the boy is. The metaphor of jail highlights the loss of freedom, not the gain of a support network or moral guidance.
